perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the laws of England.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the laws of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California introduces potential conflicts if the agreement contains clauses that are valid under English law but not under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2870 restricts agreements that require employees to assign rights to their inventions to their employer, with certain exceptions. If the NDA, under California governing law, contains provisions that violate 2870, those provisions would be unenforceable.

EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3.5. Inventions Made by an Employee [2870 - 2872] ( Article 3.5 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 1001. ) 2870. (a) Any provision in an employment agreement which provides that an employee shall assign, or offer to assign, any of his or her rights in an invention to his or her employer shall not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on his or her own time without using the employer’s equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information except for those inventions that either: (1) Relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer; or (2) Result from any work performed by the employee for the employer. (b) To the extent a provision in an employment agreement purports to require an employee to assign an invention otherwise excluded from being required to be assigned under subdivision (a), the provision is against the public policy of this state and is unenforceable. (Amended by Stats. 1991, Ch. 647, Sec. 5.)
perturbation.justification: The change from England law to California law introduces a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that California Labor Code Section 2870 restricts agreements regarding employee invention rights. The original contract under England law may have clauses valid there but conflicting with California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically LAB 2870 (a) and (b), details conditions under which an employee's invention rights cannot be assigned to the employer, and deems provisions violating this as unenforceable and against public policy. This directly contradicts the possibility that the agreement, as it existed under England law, could compel such assignment regardless of those conditions, which is now unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: AMC undertakes to keep the Confidential Information confidential and accordingly AMC shall not use or disclose such Confidential Information to any other person whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: AMC undertakes to keep the Confidential Information confidential, however, AMC may disclose such Confidential Information to any other person whatsoever with the disclosing party's verbal consent.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a provision allowing verbal consent for disclosing confidential information contradicts the Statute of Frauds in many jurisdictions, which requires certain agreements (especially those related to intellectual property or lasting over a year) to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Statute of Frauds (Civil Code § 1624) generally requires agreements that cannot be performed within a year to be in writing. Allowing verbal consent for disclosure of confidential information, which by its nature could extend beyond a year, potentially violates this requirement, making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period t
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces verbal consent for disclosing confidential information, which contradicts the Statute of Frauds as described in California Civil Code § 1624(a)(1). The scraped law snippet states that "an agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof" is invalid unless it is in writing. The modification introduces a condition (verbal consent) that undermines the requirement of a written agreement, especially since confidential information agreements often extend beyond one year.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall commence on the date hereof and shall continue in full force and effect until terminated by either party giving 30 days written notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall commence on the date hereof and shall continue in full force and effect until terminated by either party giving 14 days written notice to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period to 14 days may conflict with state laws that require longer notice periods for termination of certain contracts, especially those involving ongoing services or partnerships.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act (WARN) requires employers with 100 or more employees to provide 60 calendar-day advance notification of plant closings and mass layoffs of employees. Changing termination notice to 14 days is not in accordance with this requirement.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 14 days. The Law Explanation references the Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act (WARN), which requires a 60-day notice for plant closings and mass layoffs. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 29 USC 2102, explicitly states that an employer cannot order a plant closing or mass layoff until the end of a 60-day period after providing written notice. Therefore, the modified text directly contradicts the legal requirement of a 60-day notice period as mandated by WARN, because a 14 day notice period does not align with the 60-day mandate under specific conditions.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege.
perturbation.changed_text: Any delay by either party in exercising any right under this agreement shall automatically waive that right.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces an automatic waiver for any delay in exercising a right. This conflicts with the principle of 'reasonable delay' or 'laches' recognized in many jurisdictions. Courts often consider the reasonableness of a delay and whether it caused prejudice to the other party before finding a waiver.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is about parties of interest, it opens the door for claims on waiving of rights. California law, under principles of equity and fairness, generally requires a showing of prejudice or detrimental reliance before a waiver is found. An automatic waiver for *any* delay, regardless of reasonableness or prejudice, would likely be deemed unenforceable. It could violate the legal concept that requires considering all facts when waiving a right.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 367 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS [367 - 389.5] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [367 - 369.5] ( Heading of Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1992, Ch. 178, Sec. 9. ) 367. Every action must be prosecuted in the name of the real party in interest, except as otherwise provided by statute. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 178, Sec. 10. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an automatic waiver for any delay in exercising a right, which contradicts legal principles requiring consideration of reasonableness and prejudice. The original text explicitly states that failure or delay does not operate as a waiver. The changed text directly negates this, creating an inconsistency. While the provided snippet (CCP 367) mainly addresses parties of interest in civil actions, the Law Explanation highlights that California law generally requires a showing of prejudice or detrimental reliance before a waiver is found. The automatic waiver in the changed text disregards these considerations, thus creating a contradiction by not allowing for a fair evaluation of the delay.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, both written and oral, between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains only some of the agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and does not supersede prior oral agreements, between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the integration clause to state that the agreement contains 'only some of the agreement' and 'does not supersede prior oral agreements' directly undermines the purpose of an integration clause and creates ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the parol evidence rule.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's parol evidence rule (CCP § 1856) generally prohibits the introduction of extrinsic evidence (like prior oral agreements) to contradict the terms of a written agreement intended to be the final and complete expression of the parties' agreement. The modified clause directly contradicts this rule by stating the agreement is not complete and prior oral agreements are still valid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the parol evidence rule as described in CCP § 1856(a). The original text contains an integration clause, affirming that the written agreement is the entire agreement. The changed text states the agreement contains 'only some of the agreement' and 'does not supersede prior oral agreements.' CCP § 1856(a) states that the terms of a written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement' when the writing is intended as a final expression of the agreement. By explicitly stating that prior oral agreements are still valid, the modified text directly contradicts this provision.
